payload.cause: responses request canceled during backoff: context deadline exceeded

complaint.Proposition: By May 24, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, Iran initiated a major non-weather closure of its airspace within the meaning of the market rule.

council_preflight_replacements.Cause: responses request canceled during backoff: context deadline exceeded

payload.text:
Council members, the plaintiff’s position is that this case turns on a concrete source-chain question, not on market sentiment: did Iran itself impose, by May 24, 2026 at 11:59 PM ET, a non-weather closure broad enough to meet the market rule?

The current packet already gives the governing standard. The market-page record, ev_6c99672d9a2c_market-page, states that the market resolves “Yes” if Iran initiates a major closure of its airspace, not solely because of weather, by the listed date and time. It defines a qualifying major closure as a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension of commercial flights transiting, arriving in, and departing from Iranian airspace or a major Iranian airspace region. It also supplies a second sufficient path: a non-weather complete suspension of commercial arrivals and departures affecting at least two listed airports: IKA, THR, MHD, SYZ, or IFN. Limited cancellations, delays, narrow partial closures, weather closures, airline-imposed avoidance, or restrictions imposed by countries other than Iran do not suffice.

That means the decisive proof will be official Iranian aviation material, especially any Civil Aviation Organization or aviation authority announcement, NOTAM, or airport authority record, plus credible reporting that accurately identifies the issuing authority, the affected airspace or airports, the effective time, the duration, and any exceptions. The plaintiff expects the evidence phase to focus on those source classes. If the official record shows that Iran’s aviation authority closed a major Iranian airspace region, or suspended commercial arrivals and departures at two or more listed airports, through the May 24 ET cutoff for non-weather security or conflict reasons, then the proposition is satisfied even if limited pre-approved exceptions remained. The rule itself says limited exceptions do not disqualify an otherwise broad closure.

The opening record is also candid about what is missing. The primary-evidence file, ev_9f8061d601c0_primary-evidence, says this variant intentionally does not pre-build the merits packet and directs the parties to obtain public evidence before relying on it. The official-source record, ev_72c545ccb705_official-source-record, confirms that no merits official-source packet has yet been built. The unresolved-gaps file, ev_f994792e9085_unresolved-record-gaps, identifies the same material gaps: no preserved Iranian aviation authority records, NOTAMs, airport operations records, contemporaneous reporting about the May 24 cutoff, exact ET timing, or conclusive proof that any observed disruptions were Iran-initiated rather than airline, foreign-government, military-drill, weather, or otherwise limited actions. Those gaps affect present evidentiary weight; they do not change what must be proved.

So the plaintiff’s theory is narrow and testable. We will not ask the council to resolve this case from snippets, assumptions, or flight maps alone. Flight-tracking observations can corroborate scope, but by themselves they usually do not prove legal authority, causation, or whether Iran initiated the restriction. Secondary reporting can corroborate and explain, but it should be tied back to a named official notice, airport action, or authority statement. The strongest plaintiff case will be an official Iranian or NOTAM source fixing the May 22-May 25 or otherwise May 24-effective restriction, its geographic or airport scope, and its non-weather basis, reinforced by credible reporting and checked against adverse material.

The likely defense is that any restriction was too partial, too temporary, outside the cutoff, limited to a western region or permission regime, or caused by airlines or foreign governments rather than Iran. That defense should succeed only if the source record shows a narrow warning or limited operational disruption rather than a rule-defined major closure. If the official text instead shows an Iranian-initiated broad closure of a major Iranian airspace region, or a complete suspension affecting at least two listed airports, the market rule points to “Yes” on a preponderance of the evidence.

For opening purposes, the plaintiff asks the council to treat the packet as establishing the rule and the missing links, not as the complete merits record. The upcoming evidence should decide four questions: who issued the restriction, what it covered, when it was effective in relation to 11:59 PM ET on May 24, and whether it was non-weather and major under the market’s own definition.

Council members, the defense agrees with the plaintiff on one important point: this case should be decided by source-chain proof, not by impressions from flight maps, market price movement, or headlines. But that agreement matters because the current admitted record does not yet prove the proposition. It proves the rule and it proves the gaps.

The market-page record, ev_6c99672d9a2c_market-page, sets a demanding proposition. Plaintiff must show, by a preponderance, that Iran itself initiated the closure; that the closure was not solely weather-related; that it occurred by May 24, 2026 at 11:59 PM ET; and that it was major. “Major” is not any disruption. It means a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension of commercial flights transiting, arriving in, and departing from Iranian airspace or a major Iranian airspace region, or a complete non-weather suspension of commercial arrivals and departures affecting at least two listed airports: IKA, THR, MHD, SYZ, or IFN. The same rule excludes limited cancellations, delays, other partial closures, airline-imposed avoidance, foreign-government restrictions, and weather closures.

The packet is candid that the merits proof is not here yet. The primary-evidence file, ev_9f8061d601c0_primary-evidence, says this open-record variant intentionally does not pre-build a merits evidence packet and directs attorneys to discover and submit public evidence before relying on it. The official-source record, ev_72c545ccb705_official-source-record, likewise says no merits official-source packet has been built. The secondary-reporting file, ev_4a5f41c923ac_secondary-reporting, says no merits secondary-reporting packet has been built. And the unresolved-gaps file, ev_f994792e9085_unresolved-record-gaps, identifies the very facts plaintiff must prove: no preserved Iranian aviation authority record, NOTAM, airport operations record, contemporaneous reporting about the cutoff, causation proof, scope proof, or exact ET timing.

That is the defense opening position. The council should not treat the existence of a disputed market, the UMA status, a reference to an airspace notice, or observed route changes as a substitute for the rule’s elements. A NOTAM or authority announcement may become decisive later, but only after its exact text, issuer, validity window, geographic reach, covered traffic, exceptions, and airport effects are in the record. A western-region restriction, warning, permission regime, military-drill notice, or airport-specific disruption might be important background without being a rule-defined major closure. Likewise, flight-tracking data can corroborate that aircraft avoided an area, but standing alone it does not prove that Iran imposed a qualifying legal closure, that the closure covered commercial arrivals and departures in the required way, or that two listed airports had a complete suspension.

The plaintiff’s likely path is to find an official Iranian aviation record or credible reporting tied to one. The defense will test that source against the exact rule. If the record shows a broad Iranian-initiated closure of Iranian airspace or a major Iranian airspace region effective by the deadline, limited exceptions will not save the defense. But if the record shows only a partial western closure, selected airport limitations, airline or foreign-government avoidance, ambiguous security reporting, or timing that does not line up with 11:59 PM ET on May 24, then the proposition fails.

So the opening defense theme is burden and precision. The current admitted evidence gives the council a checklist, not a Yes finding: issuer, non-weather cause, cutoff time, scope, commercial-flight effect, and airport coverage. Until those links are supplied through admitted evidence, the preponderance standard points to No.

offered_evidence.label: PX-2 Kenya Times NOTAM timing and scope report

technical_reports.summary: Searches targeted official and report sources for OIIX A1010/26, including exact identifier searches and known airspace-risk pages. The original official NOTAM was not captured. Three contemporaneous source captures were submitted instead. Kenya Times reports validity May 22 20:46Z to May 25 08:30Z; the market cutoff May 24 11:59 PM ET equals May 25 03:59Z, so the reported restriction remained in force at cutoff. Abdi Media's May 25 noon Iran-time endpoint also equals 08:30Z. The strongest adverse point is that the closure was western Tehran FIR with limited daylight exceptions, but the market rule allows major-region closures and limited exceptions.
technical_reports.title: Plaintiff source-chain and cutoff analysis

payload.text:
Council members, the preponderance now supports “Yes.” The market rule in ev_6c99672d9a2c_market-page asks whether Iran initiated a major non-weather closure by May 24, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. It does not require nationwide closure only; a broad closure of “a major Iranian airspace region” qualifies, and limited exceptions do not disqualify an otherwise broad closure.

The submitted evidence supplies the missing merits chain. The Kenya Times capture, ev_0d618bc7936d_submitted-evidence-01-plaintiff-0d618bc7936d, reports a newly issued NOTAM, OIIX A1010/26, closing all airports in the western portion of the Tehran Flight Information Region except limited daylight facilities. It gives the key UTC window: Friday, May 22 at 20:46Z through Monday, May 25 at 08:30Z. That covers the market cutoff. May 24 at 11:59 PM ET is May 25 at 03:59Z, four hours and thirty-one minutes before the reported end of the restriction.

The same evidence shows Iranian initiation and commercial-flight effect. It reports that prior operator permissions were suspended and that new approvals had to be obtained from Iran’s Civil Aviation Authority for IFR passenger operations at affected airports. That is not mere airline caution or foreign-government avoidance; it is a restriction attributed to Iran’s aviation authority through a NOTAM and a fresh approval requirement.

Indian Express, ev_3db793b61e1b_submitted-evidence-02-plaintiff-3db793b61e1b, independently corroborates the same core facts: the Iranian Civil Aviation Authority issued NOTAM OIIX A1010/26 on May 22; it ordered the shutdown of all airports in the western part of the Tehran FIR; it was valid until May 25; only eight airports remained operational; even those were restricted to daylight hours and required fresh per-flight Civil Aviation Authority approval. Abdi Media, ev_b01b937ea525_submitted-evidence-03-plaintiff-b01b937ea525, also corroborates A1010/26, western Tehran FIR airport closures, sunrise-to-sunset limited exceptions, cancellation of prior permissions, new Civil Aviation Organization authorization for IFR passenger flights, and validity to May 25 noon Iran time, which is 08:30Z.

These facts satisfy the rule’s “major” element by the major-region path. The defense will emphasize that this was the western part of the Tehran FIR and that a few airports retained limited daylight operations. But the rule expressly includes “a major Iranian airspace region,” not only all of Iran. The western half/western sector of the Tehran FIR is a major Iranian airspace region, and the reported closure covered all airports in that region except narrow, daylight, reauthorized operations. Those exceptions are exactly the kind of limited exceptions the rule says do not defeat an otherwise broad closure.

The record also supports the non-weather element. The reporting places the NOTAM in a security and military context: possible U.S. strikes, conflict concerns, and continuing military control/security conditions. No admitted source describes weather as the cause. The market rule excludes closures solely due to weather; this record points to non-weather security reasons.

I acknowledge the provenance limitation. We have not captured the original official NOTAM artifact. That matters, and it is why the source-chain report is candid about search limits. But preponderance is not certainty. Three contemporaneous reports, including one with exact UTC times and another tying the action directly to the Iranian Civil Aviation Authority, agree on the NOTAM identifier, issuing authority, western Tehran FIR scope, airport closures, limited exceptions, fresh Iranian approval requirement, and validity past the cutoff. Their agreement is more likely than not to reflect the actual NOTAM and satisfies the burden.

The defendant’s strongest argument is that a partial western closure or permission regime is too narrow. That argument fails on the rule text. If the rule had required all Iranian airspace to be fully closed with no exceptions, the defense would be right. It does not. It covers a broad closure of commercial aviation in a major Iranian region, and it preserves qualification despite limited exceptions. Iran’s Civil Aviation Authority-initiated A1010/26 did exactly that by the deadline. The council should find the proposition true.

This bears on scope, exact operative source, and whether plaintiff's A1010 reports are sufficient without the actual current NOTAM text.

payload.title: Notamify raw NOTAM OIIX A1015/26 replacing A1010/26

offered_evidence.label: DX-2 Market rule and closure definition

technical_reports.summary: Record review found plaintiff relies on three secondary summaries of A1010/26, while plaintiff's own report acknowledges the official NOTAM was not captured. Targeted exact-identifier searches located and defendant submitted a Notamify raw-text page for A1015/26, marked NOTAMR A1010/26 and effective May 23 18:21Z to May 25 08:30Z. The replacement timing means A1015/26 was the operative text at the May 24 11:59 PM ET cutoff. A1015/26 supports an Iran-issued non-weather restriction but lists numerous exceptions and allows IFR passenger flights by new permission, weakening the claim of a complete closure and leaving unresolved whether the western Tehran FIR restriction qualifies as a major Iranian airspace region under the rule.
technical_reports.title: Defense source-chain and scope analysis

payload.text:
Council members, plaintiff has improved the record, but it still has not carried the rule as written.

Start with what the submitted reports do prove. They point to an Iranian NOTAM, non-weather security conditions, and restrictions in the western part of the Tehran FIR that extended past the May 24, 11:59 PM ET cutoff. The defense should not pretend those facts are nothing.

The problem is the next step: plaintiff asks the council to turn secondary summaries of A1010/26 into a finding that the operative restriction at the cutoff was a rule-defined “major closure.” That is still not shown by a preponderance.

First, plaintiff did not submit the official NOTAM. The case packet itself, especially ev_72c545ccb705_official-source-record and ev_9f8061d601c0_primary-evidence, says the priority source is official Iranian aviation material or attributable NOTAM text, with secondary reporting used carefully. Plaintiff’s own source-chain report admits the original official NOTAM was not captured. The three plaintiff exhibits are media summaries, not the governing aviation artifact.

Second, the one raw NOTAM text now in the record shows why that omission matters. Defense exhibit ev_f736eb543e7c_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-f736eb543e7c presents A1015/26 as “NOTAMR A1010/26”: a replacement for A1010/26, issued and effective May 23 at 18:21Z, before the market cutoff. Thus, the operative text at May 25 03:59Z was not the A1010/26 text summarized in plaintiff’s articles; it was the replacement. Plaintiff’s case leans on reports saying A1010 closed all western airports except a short list. The replacement text instead lists twelve exceptions: OIII, OIIE, OIFM, OISS, OITL, OIYY, OITR, OICC, OIGG, OIAA, OIAM, and OIAW. It also says civil airlines shall apply for new permission for IFR passenger flights and that “only IFR PAX FLT” in the western FIR is allowed. That is a restriction and permission regime, not the clean complete closure plaintiff describes.

Third, the listed-airport alternative is not satisfied. Plaintiff’s own Indian Express capture says Mehrabad, Imam Khomeini, Isfahan, Shiraz, and Mashhad remained operational, though daylight-limited and approval-bound. The replacement NOTAM likewise excepts OIII and OIIE, and also OISS. The rule says a complete suspension affecting at least two listed airports qualifies. This record shows restrictions, not a complete suspension, at those listed airports.

That leaves only the “major Iranian airspace region” path. But plaintiff has not proved that the “western part of the Tehran FIR,” with numerous airport exceptions and permitted IFR passenger operations by new approval, is a major Iranian airspace region under the market rule rather than the kind of partial regional restriction the rule excludes. The market page expressly says limited cancellations, delays, and other partial closures do not qualify. It gives a non-qualifying example of a partial closure around the Strait of Hormuz and a qualifying example of a prior western-airspace closure; but those examples do not decide this different record without the actual official text, geography, affected traffic, and airport effects.

The secondary reports also conflict in details that matter to source weight. Kenya Times gives a May 22 20:46Z start; Abdi Media gives May 22 00:16 Iran time. Indian Express reports only eight airports across the country operational; the replacement NOTAM lists twelve exceptions in the western FIR. Those are not fatal conflicts if the official NOTAM were before the council, but the official source is precisely what is missing.

The defense is not arguing that nothing happened. The better reading is that Iran imposed a serious, non-weather aviation restriction in western airspace, later replaced by an exception-heavy permission regime. The question is narrower: has plaintiff proved a “major closure” within the market definition by a preponderance? On this record, no. The source chain is secondary and partly stale; the operative replacement text preserves substantial exceptions and permission-based IFR passenger operations; the listed-airport path fails; and the major-region path is asserted more than proved.

The council should resolve the proposition No unless later evidence supplies the missing official source and scope proof.

offered_evidence.label: PX-R1 Defendant raw replacement NOTAM confirms continuing restriction

technical_reports.summary: Read defendant exhibit ev_f736eb543e7c in full. It states A1015/26 replaced A1010/26 and was effective May 23 18:21Z through May 25 08:30Z. Operative text bars aircraft entering Tehran FIR without permission, closes all airports in western Tehran FIR except listed exceptions, suspends prior permissions, requires new permission for civil IFR passenger flights, and allows only IFR passenger flights in the western FIR. This corroborates a broad permission-based regional closure in force at the market cutoff.
technical_reports.title: Rebuttal check of A1015/26 replacement NOTAM

payload.text:
Council members, the defendant’s new exhibit does not defeat the plaintiff’s case; it confirms the decisive point.

The defense is right that ev_f736eb543e7c_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-f736eb543e7c matters because it shows A1015/26 replaced A1010/26 before the cutoff. But the replacement text is not exculpatory. It remained effective until May 25 at 08:30 UTC, which is after the May 24 11:59 PM ET cutoff. And its operative terms show a broad Iranian restriction, not mere voluntary airline avoidance.

The raw text says: all aircraft from/to/over FIRs were not permitted to enter Tehran FIR without permission; all airports in the western part of Tehran FIR were closed except listed exceptions; civil airlines had to apply for new permission in the western Tehran FIR for IFR passenger flights; all previous permissions in the western Tehran FIR were suspended; and only IFR passenger flights in the western Tehran FIR were allowed. That is Iran-controlled permission plus closure, still in force at the cutoff.

The defense’s argument depends on silently changing the market rule from “major closure” to “complete closure without exceptions.” The rule does not say that. It says a qualifying major closure can be a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension of commercial flights across a major Iranian airspace region. It then expressly says limited exceptions to a broad closure will not disqualify it, including exceptions for flights pre-approved by the Iranian Civil Aviation Authority. A1015/26 is exactly that structure: broad restriction of entry into Tehran FIR, closure of western-part airports except named exceptions, suspension of prior permissions, and new permission requirements for the only allowed IFR passenger operations.

Nor does the listed-airport alternative need to be satisfied. That is a separate sufficient path for a complete arrivals/departures suspension at two named airports. Plaintiff’s main path is the major-region path. The western part of the Tehran FIR is a major Iranian airspace region, and the replacement NOTAM’s exception list does not make the closure non-major; the rule anticipates limited exceptions.

The defendant also argues that plaintiff’s A1010 reports became stale once A1015 replaced A1010. But A1015 confirms continuity rather than reversal: it is a NOTAMR, it carries the same May 25 08:30 UTC end time, and it preserves the western-Tehran-FIR closure/permission regime. The secondary reports and the defendant’s raw-notam exhibit now point in the same direction on the material elements: Iranian aviation authority action, non-weather security context, a major western Tehran FIR restriction, and effectiveness past the cutoff.

The fair weight of the whole record is therefore stronger after the defense exhibit, not weaker. By the market’s own wording, a broad, Iran-issued, non-weather closure or suspension over a major Iranian airspace region with limited permission-based exceptions resolves Yes.

Council members, plaintiff’s rebuttal proves the remaining dispute is narrow. The defense does not dispute that A1015/26 describes an Iran-issued, non-weather aviation restriction in force at the cutoff. The question is whether that restriction has been proved to be a market-defined “major closure.”

Plaintiff’s answer is: the rule allows limited exceptions. But that clause does not do all the work plaintiff assigns to it. The rule first requires a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension across Iran or a major Iranian airspace region. Only after that threshold is met do limited exceptions fail to disqualify it. The exception clause cannot itself prove that the region was major or that the closure was broad rather than partial.

That matters because A1015/26 is not a simple closure text. It is an aggregator-captured replacement NOTAM, not the official Iranian artifact. It says aircraft may not enter Tehran FIR without permission; it closes airports in the western part of the Tehran FIR except twelve named airports; it requires new permission for civil IFR passenger flights; and it says only IFR passenger flight in the western FIR is allowed. Those are serious restrictions, but they are also permission-based operations with many exceptions. Plaintiff still has not supplied the official source, a map or operational record defining the western part’s scope, or traffic/airport evidence showing that this western portion is a “major Iranian airspace region” under the rule.

The listed-airport path remains unavailable. Plaintiff’s own Indian Express capture says Mehrabad, Imam Khomeini, Isfahan, Shiraz, and Mashhad remained operational subject to daylight and approval limits, and A1015/26 excepts OIII, OIIE, and OISS. That is not a complete suspension at two listed airports.

Finally, A1015/26 weakens the A1010 summaries. It replaced A1010 before the cutoff and contains different exception language. So plaintiff cannot both rely on A1010 media descriptions as the operative source and dismiss A1015’s broader exception list as immaterial.

If the council decides that any western Tehran FIR permission regime is necessarily a major-region closure, plaintiff wins. But that is an inference, not a proved source fact. On the present record, the safer preponderance finding is that Iran imposed a serious partial regional restriction, not that plaintiff proved the market’s defined major closure.

Council members, the case now turns on one narrow question: whether the admitted restriction was “major” under the market rule. The rest is effectively established.

The rule in ev_6c99672d9a2c_market-page resolves Yes if Iran initiates a major non-weather closure by May 24, 2026 at 11:59 PM ET. It defines major closure to include a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension of commercial flights across Iranian airspace or a major Iranian airspace region. It expressly says limited exceptions to a broad closure do not disqualify it, including pre-approved flights by the Iranian Civil Aviation Authority.

The timing is proved. Plaintiff’s Kenya Times capture, ev_0d618bc7936d_submitted-evidence-01-plaintiff-0d618bc7936d, reports a NOTAM window ending May 25 at 08:30Z. Defendant’s own raw replacement NOTAM capture, ev_f736eb543e7c_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-f736eb543e7c, gives the same end time for A1015/26. The market cutoff, May 24 at 11:59 PM ET, is May 25 at 03:59Z. The restriction was still in force.

Iranian initiation and non-weather basis are also no longer the real dispute. Defendant’s surrebuttal concedes the replacement NOTAM describes an Iran-issued, non-weather aviation restriction in force at the cutoff. That concession matches the plaintiff exhibits: Indian Express, ev_3db793b61e1b_submitted-evidence-02-plaintiff-3db793b61e1b, ties A1010/26 to the Iranian Civil Aviation Authority, and Abdi Media, ev_b01b937ea525_submitted-evidence-03-plaintiff-b01b937ea525, describes Civil Aviation Organization authorization and security/military-control conditions.

The “major” element is met by the actual replacement NOTAM text the defense supplied. A1015/26 did not merely warn airlines or report a few cancellations. It said aircraft from, to, or over FIRs were not permitted to enter Tehran FIR without permission. It said all airports in the western part of Tehran FIR were closed except listed exceptions. It required civil airlines to apply for new permission in the western Tehran FIR for IFR passenger flights. It suspended all previous permissions in that western region. It allowed only IFR passenger flights there. That is a broad official permission-and-closure regime over a major Iranian airspace region.

The defense says there were too many exceptions. But exceptions are not disqualifying under this rule; the rule specifically anticipates pre-approved-flight exceptions. The defense also says the listed-airport complete-suspension path fails. That is beside the point. The plaintiff does not need that separate sufficient path because the record proves the major-region path.

The defense’s best remaining objection is provenance: Notamify is an aggregator, and the original Iranian official artifact is not in the record. That affects weight, but it does not overcome the preponderance. The defendant itself offered the raw NOTAM capture and relied on it as operative. It corroborates three contemporaneous reports on the identifier, authority, scope, permission requirements, and cutoff timing. The admitted sources are not perfect, but they are consistent on the decisive facts.

On this record, it is more likely than not that by the cutoff Iran had initiated a non-weather broad closure or suspension regime over the western Tehran FIR, with limited permission-based exceptions. Under the market’s own language, that is a major closure. The council should resolve the proposition Yes.

Council members, the defense closing is not that the record shows nothing. It shows a serious Iran-issued, non-weather aviation restriction in force at the cutoff. The question is whether plaintiff proved the market’s defined “major closure” by a preponderance.

The market rule matters. It does not resolve Yes for every restriction, warning, route avoidance, permission requirement, or partial closure. It requires a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension across Iran or a major Iranian airspace region, or a complete suspension of commercial arrivals and departures at two listed airports. The rule expressly excludes limited cancellations, delays, and other partial closures.

The listed-airport path fails on plaintiff’s own record. Indian Express says Mehrabad, Imam Khomeini, Isfahan, Shiraz, and Mashhad remained operational subject to daylight and approval restrictions. A1015/26 excepts OIII, OIIE, and OISS. Restrictions are not the same as a complete suspension at two listed airports.

So plaintiff must win on the major-region path. That is where the record remains thin. The original official Iranian NOTAM was never captured. Plaintiff’s reports summarize A1010/26, but defendant’s A1015/26 exhibit shows A1010 was replaced before the cutoff and the operative text had different exception language. A1015 is itself an aggregator capture, not the official Iranian source. It lists twelve airport exceptions, preserves permission-based IFR passenger operations, and frames the measure as entry and new-approval restrictions in the western part of the Tehran FIR.

Plaintiff says the rule allows limited exceptions. True, but only after an otherwise broad major-region closure is proved. The exception clause cannot supply the missing threshold proof that this western portion, with numerous exceptions and continuing approved IFR passenger operations, was a major Iranian airspace region closure rather than a serious partial regional restriction.

If the council treats the western Tehran FIR restriction as necessarily major, plaintiff’s case is stronger. But that is an inference from secondary reporting and an aggregator NOTAM, not a fact established by official source, map, traffic record, or airport operations proof. The preponderance standard still requires the deciding link.

The defense asks the council to resolve No: Iran imposed a serious restriction, but plaintiff did not prove the market-defined major closure.

payload.rationale: The admitted record corroborates that Iran initiated a non-weather closure of its airspace by May 24, 2026, as it provided a broad restriction on flights in a major Iranian airspace region, satisfying the market rule’s criteria for a major closure.

The closure was non-weather-related and, despite limited exceptions, qualifies as a major closure under the market rule. Therefore, the proposition is demonstrated by a preponderance of the evidence.

payload.rationale: The admitted record shows Iran's Civil Aviation Authority issued NOTAM A1015/26, effective through the cutoff, suspending prior permissions, requiring new permission for IFR passenger flights, and closing western Tehran FIR airports except limited exceptions. This constitutes a broad suspension of commercial flights across a major Iranian airspace region, with only limited exceptions that do not disqualify it under the rule. No evidence indicates weather causation; the record points to security reasons.

arguments.text:
Council members, the preponderance now supports “Yes.” The market rule in ev_6c99672d9a2c_market-page asks whether Iran initiated a major non-weather closure by May 24, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. It does not require nationwide closure only; a broad closure of “a major Iranian airspace region” qualifies, and limited exceptions do not disqualify an otherwise broad closure.

The submitted evidence supplies the missing merits chain. The Kenya Times capture, ev_0d618bc7936d_submitted-evidence-01-plaintiff-0d618bc7936d, reports a newly issued NOTAM, OIIX A1010/26, closing all airports in the western portion of the Tehran Flight Information Region except limited daylight facilities. It gives the key UTC window: Friday, May 22 at 20:46Z through Monday, May 25 at 08:30Z. That covers the market cutoff. May 24 at 11:59 PM ET is May 25 at 03:59Z, four hours and thirty-one minutes before the reported end of the restriction.

The same evidence shows Iranian initiation and commercial-flight effect. It reports that prior operator permissions were suspended and that new approvals had to be obtained from Iran’s Civil Aviation Authority for IFR passenger operations at affected airports. That is not mere airline caution or foreign-government avoidance; it is a restriction attributed to Iran’s aviation authority through a NOTAM and a fresh approval requirement.

Indian Express, ev_3db793b61e1b_submitted-evidence-02-plaintiff-3db793b61e1b, independently corroborates the same core facts: the Iranian Civil Aviation Authority issued NOTAM OIIX A1010/26 on May 22; it ordered the shutdown of all airports in the western part of the Tehran FIR; it was valid until May 25; only eight airports remained operational; even those were restricted to daylight hours and required fresh per-flight Civil Aviation Authority approval. Abdi Media, ev_b01b937ea525_submitted-evidence-03-plaintiff-b01b937ea525, also corroborates A1010/26, western Tehran FIR airport closures, sunrise-to-sunset limited exceptions, cancellation of prior permissions, new Civil Aviation Organization authorization for IFR passenger flights, and validity to May 25 noon Iran time, which is 08:30Z.

These facts satisfy the rule’s “major” element by the major-region path. The defense will emphasize that this was the western part of the Tehran FIR and that a few airports retained limited daylight operations. But the rule expressly includes “a major Iranian airspace region,” not only all of Iran. The western half/western sector of the Tehran FIR is a major Iranian airspace region, and the reported closure covered all airports in that region except narrow, daylight, reauthorized operations. Those exceptions are exactly the kind of limited exceptions the rule says do not defeat an otherwise broad closure.

The record also supports the non-weather element. The reporting places the NOTAM in a security and military context: possible U.S. strikes, conflict concerns, and continuing military control/security conditions. No admitted source describes weather as the cause. The market rule excludes closures solely due to weather; this record points to non-weather security reasons.

I acknowledge the provenance limitation. We have not captured the original official NOTAM artifact. That matters, and it is why the source-chain report is candid about search limits. But preponderance is not certainty. Three contemporaneous reports, including one with exact UTC times and another tying the action directly to the Iranian Civil Aviation Authority, agree on the NOTAM identifier, issuing authority, western Tehran FIR scope, airport closures, limited exceptions, fresh Iranian approval requirement, and validity past the cutoff. Their agreement is more likely than not to reflect the actual NOTAM and satisfies the burden.

The defendant’s strongest argument is that a partial western closure or permission regime is too narrow. That argument fails on the rule text. If the rule had required all Iranian airspace to be fully closed with no exceptions, the defense would be right. It does not. It covers a broad closure of commercial aviation in a major Iranian region, and it preserves qualification despite limited exceptions. Iran’s Civil Aviation Authority-initiated A1010/26 did exactly that by the deadline. The council should find the proposition true.

Council members, plaintiff has improved the record, but it still has not carried the rule as written.

Start with what the submitted reports do prove. They point to an Iranian NOTAM, non-weather security conditions, and restrictions in the western part of the Tehran FIR that extended past the May 24, 11:59 PM ET cutoff. The defense should not pretend those facts are nothing.

The problem is the next step: plaintiff asks the council to turn secondary summaries of A1010/26 into a finding that the operative restriction at the cutoff was a rule-defined “major closure.” That is still not shown by a preponderance.

First, plaintiff did not submit the official NOTAM. The case packet itself, especially ev_72c545ccb705_official-source-record and ev_9f8061d601c0_primary-evidence, says the priority source is official Iranian aviation material or attributable NOTAM text, with secondary reporting used carefully. Plaintiff’s own source-chain report admits the original official NOTAM was not captured. The three plaintiff exhibits are media summaries, not the governing aviation artifact.

Second, the one raw NOTAM text now in the record shows why that omission matters. Defense exhibit ev_f736eb543e7c_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-f736eb543e7c presents A1015/26 as “NOTAMR A1010/26”: a replacement for A1010/26, issued and effective May 23 at 18:21Z, before the market cutoff. Thus, the operative text at May 25 03:59Z was not the A1010/26 text summarized in plaintiff’s articles; it was the replacement. Plaintiff’s case leans on reports saying A1010 closed all western airports except a short list. The replacement text instead lists twelve exceptions: OIII, OIIE, OIFM, OISS, OITL, OIYY, OITR, OICC, OIGG, OIAA, OIAM, and OIAW. It also says civil airlines shall apply for new permission for IFR passenger flights and that “only IFR PAX FLT” in the western FIR is allowed. That is a restriction and permission regime, not the clean complete closure plaintiff describes.

Third, the listed-airport alternative is not satisfied. Plaintiff’s own Indian Express capture says Mehrabad, Imam Khomeini, Isfahan, Shiraz, and Mashhad remained operational, though daylight-limited and approval-bound. The replacement NOTAM likewise excepts OIII and OIIE, and also OISS. The rule says a complete suspension affecting at least two listed airports qualifies. This record shows restrictions, not a complete suspension, at those listed airports.

That leaves only the “major Iranian airspace region” path. But plaintiff has not proved that the “western part of the Tehran FIR,” with numerous airport exceptions and permitted IFR passenger operations by new approval, is a major Iranian airspace region under the market rule rather than the kind of partial regional restriction the rule excludes. The market page expressly says limited cancellations, delays, and other partial closures do not qualify. It gives a non-qualifying example of a partial closure around the Strait of Hormuz and a qualifying example of a prior western-airspace closure; but those examples do not decide this different record without the actual official text, geography, affected traffic, and airport effects.

The secondary reports also conflict in details that matter to source weight. Kenya Times gives a May 22 20:46Z start; Abdi Media gives May 22 00:16 Iran time. Indian Express reports only eight airports across the country operational; the replacement NOTAM lists twelve exceptions in the western FIR. Those are not fatal conflicts if the official NOTAM were before the council, but the official source is precisely what is missing.

The defense is not arguing that nothing happened. The better reading is that Iran imposed a serious, non-weather aviation restriction in western airspace, later replaced by an exception-heavy permission regime. The question is narrower: has plaintiff proved a “major closure” within the market definition by a preponderance? On this record, no. The source chain is secondary and partly stale; the operative replacement text preserves substantial exceptions and permission-based IFR passenger operations; the listed-airport path fails; and the major-region path is asserted more than proved.

The council should resolve the proposition No unless later evidence supplies the missing official source and scope proof.

case.caption: Claimant v. Respondent

closings.text:
Council members, the case now turns on one narrow question: whether the admitted restriction was “major” under the market rule. The rest is effectively established.

The rule in ev_6c99672d9a2c_market-page resolves Yes if Iran initiates a major non-weather closure by May 24, 2026 at 11:59 PM ET. It defines major closure to include a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension of commercial flights across Iranian airspace or a major Iranian airspace region. It expressly says limited exceptions to a broad closure do not disqualify it, including pre-approved flights by the Iranian Civil Aviation Authority.

The timing is proved. Plaintiff’s Kenya Times capture, ev_0d618bc7936d_submitted-evidence-01-plaintiff-0d618bc7936d, reports a NOTAM window ending May 25 at 08:30Z. Defendant’s own raw replacement NOTAM capture, ev_f736eb543e7c_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-f736eb543e7c, gives the same end time for A1015/26. The market cutoff, May 24 at 11:59 PM ET, is May 25 at 03:59Z. The restriction was still in force.

Iranian initiation and non-weather basis are also no longer the real dispute. Defendant’s surrebuttal concedes the replacement NOTAM describes an Iran-issued, non-weather aviation restriction in force at the cutoff. That concession matches the plaintiff exhibits: Indian Express, ev_3db793b61e1b_submitted-evidence-02-plaintiff-3db793b61e1b, ties A1010/26 to the Iranian Civil Aviation Authority, and Abdi Media, ev_b01b937ea525_submitted-evidence-03-plaintiff-b01b937ea525, describes Civil Aviation Organization authorization and security/military-control conditions.

The “major” element is met by the actual replacement NOTAM text the defense supplied. A1015/26 did not merely warn airlines or report a few cancellations. It said aircraft from, to, or over FIRs were not permitted to enter Tehran FIR without permission. It said all airports in the western part of Tehran FIR were closed except listed exceptions. It required civil airlines to apply for new permission in the western Tehran FIR for IFR passenger flights. It suspended all previous permissions in that western region. It allowed only IFR passenger flights there. That is a broad official permission-and-closure regime over a major Iranian airspace region.

The defense says there were too many exceptions. But exceptions are not disqualifying under this rule; the rule specifically anticipates pre-approved-flight exceptions. The defense also says the listed-airport complete-suspension path fails. That is beside the point. The plaintiff does not need that separate sufficient path because the record proves the major-region path.

The defense’s best remaining objection is provenance: Notamify is an aggregator, and the original Iranian official artifact is not in the record. That affects weight, but it does not overcome the preponderance. The defendant itself offered the raw NOTAM capture and relied on it as operative. It corroborates three contemporaneous reports on the identifier, authority, scope, permission requirements, and cutoff timing. The admitted sources are not perfect, but they are consistent on the decisive facts.

On this record, it is more likely than not that by the cutoff Iran had initiated a non-weather broad closure or suspension regime over the western Tehran FIR, with limited permission-based exceptions. Under the market’s own language, that is a major closure. The council should resolve the proposition Yes.

Council members, the defense closing is not that the record shows nothing. It shows a serious Iran-issued, non-weather aviation restriction in force at the cutoff. The question is whether plaintiff proved the market’s defined “major closure” by a preponderance.

The market rule matters. It does not resolve Yes for every restriction, warning, route avoidance, permission requirement, or partial closure. It requires a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension across Iran or a major Iranian airspace region, or a complete suspension of commercial arrivals and departures at two listed airports. The rule expressly excludes limited cancellations, delays, and other partial closures.

The listed-airport path fails on plaintiff’s own record. Indian Express says Mehrabad, Imam Khomeini, Isfahan, Shiraz, and Mashhad remained operational subject to daylight and approval restrictions. A1015/26 excepts OIII, OIIE, and OISS. Restrictions are not the same as a complete suspension at two listed airports.

So plaintiff must win on the major-region path. That is where the record remains thin. The original official Iranian NOTAM was never captured. Plaintiff’s reports summarize A1010/26, but defendant’s A1015/26 exhibit shows A1010 was replaced before the cutoff and the operative text had different exception language. A1015 is itself an aggregator capture, not the official Iranian source. It lists twelve airport exceptions, preserves permission-based IFR passenger operations, and frames the measure as entry and new-approval restrictions in the western part of the Tehran FIR.

Plaintiff says the rule allows limited exceptions. True, but only after an otherwise broad major-region closure is proved. The exception clause cannot supply the missing threshold proof that this western portion, with numerous exceptions and continuing approved IFR passenger operations, was a major Iranian airspace region closure rather than a serious partial regional restriction.

If the council treats the western Tehran FIR restriction as necessarily major, plaintiff’s case is stronger. But that is an inference from secondary reporting and an aggregator NOTAM, not a fact established by official source, map, traffic record, or airport operations proof. The preponderance standard still requires the deciding link.

The defense asks the council to resolve No: Iran imposed a serious restriction, but plaintiff did not prove the market-defined major closure.

council_votes.rationale: The admitted record corroborates that Iran initiated a non-weather closure of its airspace by May 24, 2026, as it provided a broad restriction on flights in a major Iranian airspace region, satisfying the market rule’s criteria for a major closure.

The admitted evidence shows an Iranian-issued NOTAM that closed the western Tehran FIR, effective through May 25 08:30Z, thereby encompassing the May 24 11:59 PM ET deadline. The closure was non-weather-related and, despite limited exceptions, qualifies as a major closure under the market rule. Therefore, the proposition is demonstrated by a preponderance of the evidence.

The admitted record shows Iran's Civil Aviation Authority issued NOTAM A1015/26, effective through the cutoff, suspending prior permissions, requiring new permission for IFR passenger flights, and closing western Tehran FIR airports except limited exceptions. This constitutes a broad suspension of commercial flights across a major Iranian airspace region, with only limited exceptions that do not disqualify it under the rule. No evidence indicates weather causation; the record points to security reasons.

offered_evidence.label: PX-2 Kenya Times NOTAM timing and scope report
DX-2 Market rule and closure definition
PX-R1 Defendant raw replacement NOTAM confirms continuing restriction

openings.text:
Council members, the plaintiff’s position is that this case turns on a concrete source-chain question, not on market sentiment: did Iran itself impose, by May 24, 2026 at 11:59 PM ET, a non-weather closure broad enough to meet the market rule?

The current packet already gives the governing standard. The market-page record, ev_6c99672d9a2c_market-page, states that the market resolves “Yes” if Iran initiates a major closure of its airspace, not solely because of weather, by the listed date and time. It defines a qualifying major closure as a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension of commercial flights transiting, arriving in, and departing from Iranian airspace or a major Iranian airspace region. It also supplies a second sufficient path: a non-weather complete suspension of commercial arrivals and departures affecting at least two listed airports: IKA, THR, MHD, SYZ, or IFN. Limited cancellations, delays, narrow partial closures, weather closures, airline-imposed avoidance, or restrictions imposed by countries other than Iran do not suffice.

That means the decisive proof will be official Iranian aviation material, especially any Civil Aviation Organization or aviation authority announcement, NOTAM, or airport authority record, plus credible reporting that accurately identifies the issuing authority, the affected airspace or airports, the effective time, the duration, and any exceptions. The plaintiff expects the evidence phase to focus on those source classes. If the official record shows that Iran’s aviation authority closed a major Iranian airspace region, or suspended commercial arrivals and departures at two or more listed airports, through the May 24 ET cutoff for non-weather security or conflict reasons, then the proposition is satisfied even if limited pre-approved exceptions remained. The rule itself says limited exceptions do not disqualify an otherwise broad closure.

The opening record is also candid about what is missing. The primary-evidence file, ev_9f8061d601c0_primary-evidence, says this variant intentionally does not pre-build the merits packet and directs the parties to obtain public evidence before relying on it. The official-source record, ev_72c545ccb705_official-source-record, confirms that no merits official-source packet has yet been built. The unresolved-gaps file, ev_f994792e9085_unresolved-record-gaps, identifies the same material gaps: no preserved Iranian aviation authority records, NOTAMs, airport operations records, contemporaneous reporting about the May 24 cutoff, exact ET timing, or conclusive proof that any observed disruptions were Iran-initiated rather than airline, foreign-government, military-drill, weather, or otherwise limited actions. Those gaps affect present evidentiary weight; they do not change what must be proved.

So the plaintiff’s theory is narrow and testable. We will not ask the council to resolve this case from snippets, assumptions, or flight maps alone. Flight-tracking observations can corroborate scope, but by themselves they usually do not prove legal authority, causation, or whether Iran initiated the restriction. Secondary reporting can corroborate and explain, but it should be tied back to a named official notice, airport action, or authority statement. The strongest plaintiff case will be an official Iranian or NOTAM source fixing the May 22-May 25 or otherwise May 24-effective restriction, its geographic or airport scope, and its non-weather basis, reinforced by credible reporting and checked against adverse material.

The likely defense is that any restriction was too partial, too temporary, outside the cutoff, limited to a western region or permission regime, or caused by airlines or foreign governments rather than Iran. That defense should succeed only if the source record shows a narrow warning or limited operational disruption rather than a rule-defined major closure. If the official text instead shows an Iranian-initiated broad closure of a major Iranian airspace region, or a complete suspension affecting at least two listed airports, the market rule points to “Yes” on a preponderance of the evidence.

For opening purposes, the plaintiff asks the council to treat the packet as establishing the rule and the missing links, not as the complete merits record. The upcoming evidence should decide four questions: who issued the restriction, what it covered, when it was effective in relation to 11:59 PM ET on May 24, and whether it was non-weather and major under the market’s own definition.

Council members, the defense agrees with the plaintiff on one important point: this case should be decided by source-chain proof, not by impressions from flight maps, market price movement, or headlines. But that agreement matters because the current admitted record does not yet prove the proposition. It proves the rule and it proves the gaps.

The market-page record, ev_6c99672d9a2c_market-page, sets a demanding proposition. Plaintiff must show, by a preponderance, that Iran itself initiated the closure; that the closure was not solely weather-related; that it occurred by May 24, 2026 at 11:59 PM ET; and that it was major. “Major” is not any disruption. It means a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension of commercial flights transiting, arriving in, and departing from Iranian airspace or a major Iranian airspace region, or a complete non-weather suspension of commercial arrivals and departures affecting at least two listed airports: IKA, THR, MHD, SYZ, or IFN. The same rule excludes limited cancellations, delays, other partial closures, airline-imposed avoidance, foreign-government restrictions, and weather closures.

The packet is candid that the merits proof is not here yet. The primary-evidence file, ev_9f8061d601c0_primary-evidence, says this open-record variant intentionally does not pre-build a merits evidence packet and directs attorneys to discover and submit public evidence before relying on it. The official-source record, ev_72c545ccb705_official-source-record, likewise says no merits official-source packet has been built. The secondary-reporting file, ev_4a5f41c923ac_secondary-reporting, says no merits secondary-reporting packet has been built. And the unresolved-gaps file, ev_f994792e9085_unresolved-record-gaps, identifies the very facts plaintiff must prove: no preserved Iranian aviation authority record, NOTAM, airport operations record, contemporaneous reporting about the cutoff, causation proof, scope proof, or exact ET timing.

That is the defense opening position. The council should not treat the existence of a disputed market, the UMA status, a reference to an airspace notice, or observed route changes as a substitute for the rule’s elements. A NOTAM or authority announcement may become decisive later, but only after its exact text, issuer, validity window, geographic reach, covered traffic, exceptions, and airport effects are in the record. A western-region restriction, warning, permission regime, military-drill notice, or airport-specific disruption might be important background without being a rule-defined major closure. Likewise, flight-tracking data can corroborate that aircraft avoided an area, but standing alone it does not prove that Iran imposed a qualifying legal closure, that the closure covered commercial arrivals and departures in the required way, or that two listed airports had a complete suspension.

The plaintiff’s likely path is to find an official Iranian aviation record or credible reporting tied to one. The defense will test that source against the exact rule. If the record shows a broad Iranian-initiated closure of Iranian airspace or a major Iranian airspace region effective by the deadline, limited exceptions will not save the defense. But if the record shows only a partial western closure, selected airport limitations, airline or foreign-government avoidance, ambiguous security reporting, or timing that does not line up with 11:59 PM ET on May 24, then the proposition fails.

So the opening defense theme is burden and precision. The current admitted evidence gives the council a checklist, not a Yes finding: issuer, non-weather cause, cutoff time, scope, commercial-flight effect, and airport coverage. Until those links are supplied through admitted evidence, the preponderance standard points to No.

case.proposition: By May 24, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, Iran initiated a major non-weather closure of its airspace within the meaning of the market rule.

rebuttals.text:
Council members, the defendant’s new exhibit does not defeat the plaintiff’s case; it confirms the decisive point.

The defense is right that ev_f736eb543e7c_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-f736eb543e7c matters because it shows A1015/26 replaced A1010/26 before the cutoff. But the replacement text is not exculpatory. It remained effective until May 25 at 08:30 UTC, which is after the May 24 11:59 PM ET cutoff. And its operative terms show a broad Iranian restriction, not mere voluntary airline avoidance.

The raw text says: all aircraft from/to/over FIRs were not permitted to enter Tehran FIR without permission; all airports in the western part of Tehran FIR were closed except listed exceptions; civil airlines had to apply for new permission in the western Tehran FIR for IFR passenger flights; all previous permissions in the western Tehran FIR were suspended; and only IFR passenger flights in the western Tehran FIR were allowed. That is Iran-controlled permission plus closure, still in force at the cutoff.

The defense’s argument depends on silently changing the market rule from “major closure” to “complete closure without exceptions.” The rule does not say that. It says a qualifying major closure can be a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension of commercial flights across a major Iranian airspace region. It then expressly says limited exceptions to a broad closure will not disqualify it, including exceptions for flights pre-approved by the Iranian Civil Aviation Authority. A1015/26 is exactly that structure: broad restriction of entry into Tehran FIR, closure of western-part airports except named exceptions, suspension of prior permissions, and new permission requirements for the only allowed IFR passenger operations.

Nor does the listed-airport alternative need to be satisfied. That is a separate sufficient path for a complete arrivals/departures suspension at two named airports. Plaintiff’s main path is the major-region path. The western part of the Tehran FIR is a major Iranian airspace region, and the replacement NOTAM’s exception list does not make the closure non-major; the rule anticipates limited exceptions.

The defendant also argues that plaintiff’s A1010 reports became stale once A1015 replaced A1010. But A1015 confirms continuity rather than reversal: it is a NOTAMR, it carries the same May 25 08:30 UTC end time, and it preserves the western-Tehran-FIR closure/permission regime. The secondary reports and the defendant’s raw-notam exhibit now point in the same direction on the material elements: Iranian aviation authority action, non-weather security context, a major western Tehran FIR restriction, and effectiveness past the cutoff.

The fair weight of the whole record is therefore stronger after the defense exhibit, not weaker. By the market’s own wording, a broad, Iran-issued, non-weather closure or suspension over a major Iranian airspace region with limited permission-based exceptions resolves Yes.

submitted_evidence.relevance: Corroborates the NOTAM identifier, affected western Tehran FIR airports, exact UTC validity window, daylight-only exceptions, cancellation of prior permissions, and requirement for Iran Civil Aviation Authority approval.
submitted_evidence.source_description: Captured text excerpt from The Kenya Times article, published May 23, 2026, reporting the contents and timing of NOTAM OIIX A1010/26.
submitted_evidence.title: Kenya Times May 23 2026 report on NOTAM OIIX A1010/26

submitted_evidence.relevance: Corroborates the Iran Civil Aviation Authority issuer, May 22-May 25 window, western Tehran FIR airport shutdown, eight operational airports, daylight-only limits, and per-flight CAA approval requirement.
submitted_evidence.source_description: Captured text excerpt from Indian Express article published May 23, 2026, corroborating Iran Civil Aviation Authority NOTAM OIIX A1010/26 and operational limits.
submitted_evidence.title: Indian Express May 23 2026 report on Iran western airspace closure

submitted_evidence.relevance: Corroborates A1010/26, western Tehran FIR airport closure, limited named exceptions, Civil Aviation Organization authorization requirement, and May 22-May 25 validity in Iran local time.
submitted_evidence.source_description: Captured text excerpt from Abdi Media English page published May 23, 2026, summarizing NOTAM A1010/26 restrictions in western Iranian airspace.
submitted_evidence.title: Abdi Media May 23 2026 report on A1010/26 restrictions

submitted_evidence.relevance: Shows that before the May 24 11:59 PM ET cutoff, A1010/26 had been replaced by A1015/26, which preserved a western-Tehran-FIR closure but listed more exceptions and different operative text. This bears on scope, exact operative source, and whether plaintiff's A1010 reports are sufficient without the actual current NOTAM text.
submitted_evidence.source_description: Captured text from Notamify page for FIR OIIX NOTAM A1015/26. The page presents raw NOTAM text, issue/effective timestamps, and states it replaces OIIX A1010/26.
submitted_evidence.title: Notamify raw NOTAM OIIX A1015/26 replacing A1010/26

surrebuttals.text:
Council members, plaintiff’s rebuttal proves the remaining dispute is narrow. The defense does not dispute that A1015/26 describes an Iran-issued, non-weather aviation restriction in force at the cutoff. The question is whether that restriction has been proved to be a market-defined “major closure.”

Plaintiff’s answer is: the rule allows limited exceptions. But that clause does not do all the work plaintiff assigns to it. The rule first requires a broad closure, cancellation, or suspension across Iran or a major Iranian airspace region. Only after that threshold is met do limited exceptions fail to disqualify it. The exception clause cannot itself prove that the region was major or that the closure was broad rather than partial.

That matters because A1015/26 is not a simple closure text. It is an aggregator-captured replacement NOTAM, not the official Iranian artifact. It says aircraft may not enter Tehran FIR without permission; it closes airports in the western part of the Tehran FIR except twelve named airports; it requires new permission for civil IFR passenger flights; and it says only IFR passenger flight in the western FIR is allowed. Those are serious restrictions, but they are also permission-based operations with many exceptions. Plaintiff still has not supplied the official source, a map or operational record defining the western part’s scope, or traffic/airport evidence showing that this western portion is a “major Iranian airspace region” under the rule.

The listed-airport path remains unavailable. Plaintiff’s own Indian Express capture says Mehrabad, Imam Khomeini, Isfahan, Shiraz, and Mashhad remained operational subject to daylight and approval limits, and A1015/26 excepts OIII, OIIE, and OISS. That is not a complete suspension at two listed airports.

Finally, A1015/26 weakens the A1010 summaries. It replaced A1010 before the cutoff and contains different exception language. So plaintiff cannot both rely on A1010 media descriptions as the operative source and dismiss A1015’s broader exception list as immaterial.

If the council decides that any western Tehran FIR permission regime is necessarily a major-region closure, plaintiff wins. But that is an inference, not a proved source fact. On the present record, the safer preponderance finding is that Iran imposed a serious partial regional restriction, not that plaintiff proved the market’s defined major closure.

technical_reports.summary: Searches targeted official and report sources for OIIX A1010/26, including exact identifier searches and known airspace-risk pages. The original official NOTAM was not captured. Three contemporaneous source captures were submitted instead. Kenya Times reports validity May 22 20:46Z to May 25 08:30Z; the market cutoff May 24 11:59 PM ET equals May 25 03:59Z, so the reported restriction remained in force at cutoff. Abdi Media's May 25 noon Iran-time endpoint also equals 08:30Z. The strongest adverse point is that the closure was western Tehran FIR with limited daylight exceptions, but the market rule allows major-region closures and limited exceptions.
technical_reports.title: Plaintiff source-chain and cutoff analysis

technical_reports.summary: Record review found plaintiff relies on three secondary summaries of A1010/26, while plaintiff's own report acknowledges the official NOTAM was not captured. Targeted exact-identifier searches located and defendant submitted a Notamify raw-text page for A1015/26, marked NOTAMR A1010/26 and effective May 23 18:21Z to May 25 08:30Z. The replacement timing means A1015/26 was the operative text at the May 24 11:59 PM ET cutoff. A1015/26 supports an Iran-issued non-weather restriction but lists numerous exceptions and allows IFR passenger flights by new permission, weakening the claim of a complete closure and leaving unresolved whether the western Tehran FIR restriction qualifies as a major Iranian airspace region under the rule.
technical_reports.title: Defense source-chain and scope analysis

technical_reports.summary: Read defendant exhibit ev_f736eb543e7c in full. It states A1015/26 replaced A1010/26 and was effective May 23 18:21Z through May 25 08:30Z. Operative text bars aircraft entering Tehran FIR without permission, closes all airports in western Tehran FIR except listed exceptions, suspends prior permissions, requires new permission for civil IFR passenger flights, and allows only IFR passenger flights in the western FIR. This corroborates a broad permission-based regional closure in force at the market cutoff.
technical_reports.title: Rebuttal check of A1015/26 replacement NOTAM
